Article Title: Siri AI, Visual Intelligence and 6 key Siri features announced at Apple WWDC 2026
Article Text:
Key features of the 'new' Siri AI



Deep personal context:

Onscreen awareness:

New ways to access:

A dedicated

Siri app

:

Visual Intelligence and smarter tools



Privacy at the core







Apple has officially introduced AI-powered Siri, which the company says has been developed from ground-up, and reimagines the voice assistant powered by the next generation of Apple Intelligence foundation models. Available to developers starting today, with a public beta arriving later this year, Siri AI represents the most significant upgrade to Siri since its original launch. According to the tech giant, the upgraded assistant features -- powered by the same underlying technology that powers Google's Gemini models -- offer massive improvements in conversational reasoning, deep onscreen awareness and a sharp understanding of a user's personal context. Here is everything that is new:The AI-updated Siri has four key features, which include:Siri AI can securely browse your personal data across Apple apps, instantly pull up a specific recommendations from a past text thread, for example, find an old hotel confirmation email, or locate specific photos from a family vacation.Moreover, Siri AI moves beyond simple commands to offer natural, back-and-forth conversation where users can ask follow-up questions without starting over each time.The second big change is awareness on what’s on the user’s screen. The assistant tracks what is currently happening on your display. For example, if you receive a text message about a potluck dinner, you can ask Siri AI to brainstorm recipe ideas based on that text and automatically save them to your Notes app.iPhone users can now trigger a deep system conversation by swiping down directly from the Dynamic Island or pressing the side button.Apple is launching a standalone Siri app that automatically logs and syncs your conversational history via iCloud, allowing you to start a chat on a Mac and pick it up later on your iPhone or Apple Watch. Also integrated across CarPlay, and AirPods for on-the-go use.Siri Camera Mode: On iPhone, a new specialised mode in the Camera app lets Siri analyse the physical world. For example, users can click a photo to instantly split a dinner bill via Apple Cash or get nutritional facts about a plate of food.Expanded ecosystem support: Visual Intelligence expands to Mac via keyboard shortcuts, iPad through the screenshot tool and Apple Vision Pro by allowing users to simply look at physical objects or open windows to ask questions.Systemwide writing tools: Siri AI can now generate text drafts from scratch or edit existing work based on user descriptions. In Mail and Messages, it analyses past conversations to automatically match the specific tone and punctuation you normally use with a specific recipient.Apple says Siri AI is built on an architecture specifically designed to protect user privacy, with on-device processing handling sensitive personal data wherever possible. According to Craig Federighi, Apple’s Senior Vice President of Software Engineering, the upgrade allows users to take action across their favourite apps far more naturally than ever before.
Article Summary: Siri AI available to developers starting today, with a public beta arriving later this year. Powered by the same underlying technology that powers Google's Gemini models. Offers massive improvements in conversational reasoning, deep onscreen awareness and a sharp understanding of a user's personal context. Siri AI can securely browse your personal data across Apple apps.

Article Title: Microsoft is changing its Xbox exclusive strategy under new boss Asha Sharma: What COO Matt Booty said
Article Text:
Multiplayer stays everywhere but exclusives are back







Microsoft is changing its approach to video game exclusivity under its newly appointed Xbox CEO, Asha Sharma. Following the company’s recent Xbox Games Showcase, high-ranking executives are opening up about how Microsoft plans to balance keeping its biggest games on Xbox while still launching titles on competing consoles like the PlayStation 5.Xbox Chief Content Officer (CCO) Matt Booty has clarified exactly what that means for players going forward. Speaking with Gamertag Radio, Booty said, “Our big multiplayer games [and] live-service games are going to continue to be multiplatform.”His comments came soon after the high-stakes event which marked the first major showcase under Sharma, who took the helm with a pledge to “reset” the struggling gaming division. Sharma has explicitly stated that Microsoft “must” have exclusive content to revitalize the Xbox brand and boost lagging console sales.Booty emphasized that Microsoft has not forgotten its core fanbase.“We want people to have a reason to get on board with Xbox. We want them to have a reason to buy an Xbox... At the same time, we want to reward all of our players who have been with us for a long time... we know that exclusives are important,” he added.Following the showcase, Microsoft published a blog post explicitly touting a “return to exclusives”. The company confirmed that highly anticipated upcoming titles like Gears of War: E-Day and Clockwork Revolution are permanent Xbox console exclusives, rather than “timed exclusives” that eventually migrate to PlayStation.Despite the aggressive pivot back to traditional exclusives, Booty admitted that Microsoft’s strategy is still fluid and will be handled on a game-by-game basis.“We’re going to keep thinking about this going forward," Booty said, explaining that Microsoft wants to make the “right decision, not the fast decision” for each individual release.“If we promised something to players, we’re going to honor that promise,” he said, noting that when a game's release date is officially announced, the platforms will be locked in so players “know what the choice will be.” Any games previously promised for multiplatform releases will stick to that plan.
Article Summary: Microsoft is changing its approach to video game exclusivity under its newly appointed Xbox CEO, Asha Sharma. Xbox Chief Content Officer Matt Booty has clarified exactly what that means for players going forward. “Our big multiplayer games [and] live-service games are going to continue to be multiplatform,” he said.

Article Title: Inside Apple's secret 'failure meeting' that made the company surrender to Google
Article Text:
Apple's pivot to Google's Gemini for Siri traces back to a tense early-2025 executive meeting, Bloomberg's Mark Gurman reports in his Power On newsletter. With Tim Cook absent, senior leaders confronted the Apple Intelligence flop and Siri delays. Vision Pro creator Mike Rockwell volunteered to fix Siri, AI chief John Giannandrea was sidelined, and Apple ultimately handed Siri's underlying models to Google ahead of WWDC 2026.

Why Mike Rockwell didn't get the AI crown he wanted at Apple

The Google Gemini deal that ended Apple's AI isolation





Sometime in early 2025, Apple 's most senior executives squeezed into a conference room near Craig Federighi's software engineering wing to face an uncomfortable truth—Apple Intelligence was flopping, the big Siri overhaul was about to slip, and rivals from OpenAI to Google had quietly run laps around Cupertino. Tim Cook wasn't in the room. Then-COO Jeff Williams called the meeting to order. By the time everyone walked out, Apple had started drafting the recommendation that would eventually push the world's most secretive hardware company into Google's arms.The account comes from Bloomberg's Mark Gurman, who detailed the meeting in the latest edition of his Power On newsletter ahead of WWDC 2026.Vision Pro creator Mike Rockwell emerged as the unlikely fixer in that room. Passionate about AI, he volunteered to take over Siri and the wider effort. Cook had reportedly lost confidence in then-AI chief John Giannandrea, who was sitting at the same table. Federighi and now-hardware chief Johny Srouji backed Rockwell for the Siri job.The handover wasn't clean. Rockwell thought he was signing up to lead all of Apple AI, reporting directly to Cook. Federighi pushed back. He wanted software engineering to keep Siri and AI under its roof, with Rockwell reporting to him.Rockwell briefly walked away, telling colleagues Federighi had been slow to take AI seriously. He eventually accepted. Giannandrea was stripped of most of his portfolio and left this year. Former Google and Microsoft executive Amar Subramanya was later hired to run AI models and research under Federighi.Once installed, Rockwell concluded Siri's leadership was subpar and Apple's in-house models trailed the industry. With Federighi and services chief Eddy Cue, he helped negotiate a deal to power Siri with Google's Gemini models and Google Cloud—a remarkable concession from a company that prefers to build its own silicon and software stack. Apple may swing back to its own models eventually, Gurman writes, but Gemini will do the heavy lifting at launch.Cook, normally hands-off on product roadmaps, got personally involved after the Apple Intelligence flop. He delivered an internal AI pep talk and began making feature calls himself. Federighi, who previously dismissed the need for chatbots and AI apps, has reversed course. On Monday, he takes the WWDC 2026 stage to unveil iOS 27, a chatbot-style Siri, and a standalone Siri app pitched as a ChatGPT rival.It will also be Cook's final WWDC as CEO. John Ternus takes over on September 1.
Article Summary: Apple's pivot to Google's Gemini for Siri traces back to a tense early-2025 executive meeting. With Tim Cook absent, senior leaders confronted the Apple Intelligence flop and Siri delays. Vision Pro creator Mike Rockwell volunteered to fix Siri, AI chief John Giannandrea was sidelined. Apple ultimately handed Siri's underlying models to Google ahead of WWDC 2026.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Steve Jobs: ‘The only way to do great work is…’
Article Text:
Quote of the day by Steve Jobs



“The only way to do great work is to

love what you do

. If you haven't found it yet, keep looking. Don't settle.”

What the quote reveals about motivation



Why “keep looking” is the most important part



“If you haven't found it yet, keep looking.

Don't settle.”

The idea reflects Jobs’ own journey



What the other quotes add to the picture



"You can’t connect the dots looking forward; you can only connect them looking backwards.”

“keep looking.”

“Your time is limited, so don’t waste it living someone else’s life.”

The quote may also raise difficult questions



Other famous quotes by Steve Jobs



“Your time is limited, so don’t waste it living someone else’s life.”

“You can’t connect the dots looking forward; you can only connect them looking backwards.”

“Innovation distinguishes between a leader and a follower.”

“Stay hungry. Stay foolish.”

“Sometimes life hits you in the head with a brick. Don’t lose faith.”

Why this quote continues to resonate





Some advice becomes popular because it is practical. Other advice endures because it asks people to think differently about their choices. This quote from Steve Jobs belongs to the second category. It does not offer a shortcut to success or a formula for achievement. Instead, it focuses on a question many people spend years trying to answer: What kind of work is worth committing your life to?Jobs built products, led companies through setbacks, and returned to rebuild one after being pushed out of it. Through those experiences, he often spoke about purpose, curiosity, and persistence. This quote captures all three ideas in a few simple sentences.At first glance, the quote seems straightforward. People tend to do better work when they enjoy what they are doing. But Jobs was pointing to something deeper than job satisfaction.Work often involves repetition, setbacks, and uncertainty. When someone genuinely cares about what they are building, creating, or solving, they are more likely to continue when progress is slow. Passion does not remove obstacles, but it can change how people respond to them.That may be why Jobs linked meaningful work with love rather than talent. Skills can be learned and experience can be gained. Sustained commitment is often harder to develop without a personal connection to the work itself.The first sentence is the one most people remember. The second may be the one that matters more.Those words acknowledge that many people do not immediately discover work that feels meaningful. Careers often develop through trial and error, unexpected opportunities, and changing interests.Jobs is not suggesting that everyone should quit their job tomorrow in search of a perfect calling. Instead, he seems to be encouraging curiosity. The quote recognises that finding the right fit can take time and that settling too quickly may lead to years spent pursuing something that never feels fully engaging.The quote carries additional weight because it mirrors Jobs’ personal story. In 1985, he left Apple after a power struggle within the company he had co-founded. He later launched NeXT and acquired the animation studio that became Pixar. More than a decade later, he returned to Apple and helped guide its transformation through products such as the iMac, iPod, iPhone and iPad.Looking back, Jobs often described that difficult period as one that ultimately pushed him toward new opportunities. His willingness to continue exploring different paths reflects the message behind the quote: finding meaningful work is not always a direct process.Jobs frequently returned to the idea of following curiosity and trusting long-term instincts. One of his most cited observations was,That idea aligns closely withBoth suggest that people rarely have complete certainty about where a decision will lead. Progress often becomes clearer only in hindsight.Another quote offers a similar perspective:Together, these remarks form a broader philosophy. Jobs believed that personal conviction matters because time and attention are limited resources. Spending them on something that feels meaningful is not simply a career choice; it is a life choice.Not everyone finds this advice easy to accept. Some people may argue that work is primarily about stability, responsibility, or providing for a family. Others may point out that many essential jobs are not driven by passion but by necessity. Those perspectives are valid.Loving what you do is not always possible in every stage of life. Circumstances often require practical decisions. The quote does not erase those realities.What it does suggest is that people should remain open to discovering work that feels more aligned with their interests and values. Even if that search takes years, Jobs believed it was worth continuing.Many motivational quotes focus on outcomes. They talk about success, achievement, or recognition. This one focuses on the relationship between a person and their work.It does not promise rewards. It does not guarantee results. Instead, it argues that meaningful effort begins with genuine interest and that settling too quickly can prevent people from finding it.People may interpret the quote differently depending on where they are in life. Some may see it as encouragement. Others may view it as a challenge. Either way, it asks a question that remains relevant long after it is read. Are you doing work you care about? And if not, are you still looking?
Article Summary: Steve Jobs' quote: 'The only way to do great work is to love what you do' Jobs often spoke about purpose, curiosity, and persistence. The quote recognises that finding the right fit can take time and that settling too quickly may lead to years spent pursuing something that never feels fully engaging.

Article Title: Technology sector’s ‘hottest companies' lost $1.3 trillion in less than 24 hours: What wiped hundreds of billion dollars from market cap of Nvidia, Micron, Broadcom and other chip stocks
Article Text:
What sparked the trillion-dollar wipeout







The artificial intelligence (AI) boom suffered a massive blow as the technology sector’s hottest chip companies saw a staggering $1.3 trillion in market value vanish in less than 24 hours. The massive selloff triggered the deepest one-day drop for the PHLX chip index (.SOX) since March 2020, when the onset of the coronavirus pandemic threw global markets into disruption.The panic swept through Wall Street's favourite AI heavyweights, leaving massive financial damage in its wake, news agency Reuters reported. Nvidia, the world’s most dominant AI chipmaker plunged 6%, wiping out more than $300 billion from its market capitalisation.Micron Technology tumbled 13%, evaporating roughly $150 billion in market value while Marvell Technology suffered a 17% collapse. Lisa Su-led Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) dropped nearly 11%, and Broadcom slumped 7.9%, bringing its devastating two-day losses close to 20%.The financial bleeding actually began on Thursday (June 4) after Broadcom released its quarterly financial report. While Broadcom has been one of the biggest winners of the AI gold rush, its latest numbers showed that demand for its custom AI chips fell short of Wall Street’s expectations.Broadcom’s weak report echoed across the financial world, fueling investor anxieties that the expensive AI trade may have grown too detached from reality, the report said, adding, that for over a year, tech investors have operated under a highly profitable strategy: "buy the dip" whenever tech stocks saw a slight decline. On Friday, that strategy officially hit a wall.“You've had a lot of people here that were just blindly buying the dip. Blindly buying the dip had been winning you money, but that ended today,” said Dennis Dick, a proprietary trader at Triple D Trading.The timing of the market correction is especially tense for Silicon Valley. The two-day chip rout comes just as Elon Musk prepares to launch a blockbuster initial public offering (IPO) next week for SpaceX at an exceedingly high $1.75 trillion valuation.
Article Summary: The tech sector's hottest chip companies saw a staggering $1.3 trillion in market value vanish in less than 24 hours. The selloff triggered the deepest one-day drop for the PHLX chip index (.SOX) since March 2020. The panic swept through Wall Street's favourite AI heavyweights, leaving massive financial damage in its wake.

Article Title: KPMG survey just showed why more than 50% companies may be in for a 'rude shock' on their AI spending
Article Text:
CFOs face growing AI bills

Firms take measures to control spending





Companies rushing to adopt AI may soon face unexpected costs as many struggle to track how much they are actually spending on the technology, says a KPMG survey. The study found that only 26% of companies have a comprehensive view of their AI costs, while 50% have only partial visibility. Another 22% said they have no visibility into their AI spending or only see costs after receiving bills. Unlike traditional software subscriptions that often come with fixed fees, many AI services charge customers based on usage. Costs are measured through "tokens," which are units used to calculate how much computing power AI systems consume.According to Steve Chase, KPMG's global head of AI, many companies are struggling to keep up with the rapid growth in usage."It's a new resource that needs to be managed that didn't exist quite that way, and we're seeing exponential growth," Chase said as quoted in a report by The Wall Street Journal.KPMG said it is working with companies that exhausted their annual token and cloud-computing budgets within months. One client saw its token usage increase sixfold, according to Chase.The shift to usage-based pricing is forcing finance leaders to monitor AI consumption more closely.Gil Luria, head of technology research at D.A. Davidson, said the model places more responsibility on customers."Many other CFOs are going to see their Anthropic bill and freak out this quarter," Luria said.Companies including OpenAI, Anthropic, Microsoft and Salesforce charge enterprise customers at least partly based on how much they use AI services.Several businesses are already taking steps to control spending. As per the WSJ report, Digital safety company Life360 is redesigning AI agents and using tools that reduce token consumption. Buy-now-pay-later company Affirm said its AI usage increased sharply after software teams began using AI agents to write more code.Amer Sports, the company behind brands such as Arc'teryx and Salomon, is also taking a measured approach."I didn't want everyone going out, you know, using tokens and setting up AI agents or setting up AI processes that didn't have long-term scalable benefits for the entire back-end process," finance chief Andrew Page said, per the report.
Article Summary: Only 26% of companies have a comprehensive view of their AI costs, says KPMG. 22% said they have no visibility into their AI spending or only see costs after receiving bills. Unlike traditional software subscriptions, many AI services charge customers based on usage.

Article Title: From 1948's Manchester Baby to early Android, the Virtual OS museum runs the entire history of computing
Article Text:
The list goes far past Windows 95 nostalgia





There exists a Virtual OS Museum, and it is exactly what it sounds like. Andrew Warkentin, a developer who has been quietly collecting emulator images since 2003, has packaged the lot into a Linux VM that boots over 1,700 pre-installed operating systems on a normal laptop. The span runs from 1948's Manchester Baby, the first stored-program computer, through to early Android and iOS—570 distinct OSes across more than 250 platforms.The point of the project is that none of it is fiddly. Emulators, install media, device configs, version-specific regressions—Warkentin has done that work. You open the launcher, pick an entry, it runs. Break something, roll it back with a snapshot. The VM sits on QEMU, VirtualBox, or UTM, and installers for Windows, macOS, and Linux ship in the box.Most of the famous names turn up. Windows 1.0 through early Longhorn betas. Classic Mac OS through Mac OS X 10.5 PPC. OS/2, BeOS, NeXTSTEP, PalmOS, Newton OS. The weirder corners are the fun ones, though—TempleOS, Plan 9, QNX, ZetaLisp, Oberon, plus mainframe relics like CTSS, MVS, Multics, and TOPS-10/20. Home computer heads get the BBC Micro, ZX Spectrum, Commodore 8-bits, MSX, Atari 8-bit. Xerox Star Pilot, often called the first OS with a desktop-metaphor GUI, is in.So is software written for the Manchester Baby itself.What you do not get is a stocked machine. Installations ship with whatever shipped with the OS originally—calculators, file managers, text editors. Sourcing apps for CTSS in 2026 is your problem.The asking price is disk space. The full offline edition is 121GB zipped, 174GB unpacked. A 14GB lite version pulls images on first boot and is the saner pick for most people.Two things worth flagging. The host VM is x86-only, so Apple silicon and ARM machines will run it slowly. And Warkentin calls this a preliminary release, with some OSes only working in specific emulator builds.
Article Summary: Virtual OS Museum is a Linux VM that boots over 1,700 pre-installed operating systems. The list runs from 1948's Manchester Baby, the first stored-program computer, through to early Android and iOS. The VM sits on QEMU, VirtualBox, or UTM, and installers for Windows, macOS, and Linux ship in the box.

Article Title: ‘America’s largest private employer tells its employees AI will not take their job’
Article Text:
How AI is being used across Walmart’s business





Walmart has told its more than 2.1 million employees that AI is meant to improve their jobs, not replace them, reports Financial Times. Speaking at Walmart's annual Associates Week gathering in Arkansas, company leaders said technology will play a bigger role in the future of work, but employees will remain at the center of the business. The comments come at a time when concerns are growing about AI's impact on jobs across industries. As quoted in the report, addressing thousands of workers, Walmart Chief People Officer Donna Morris said the company's future would be shaped by both technology and people."Technology will power our future. But our associates will lead it," Morris said.Executives used the event to highlight how AI is already being used across the company. Walmart recently announced that any US employee can now get certified in the use of OpenAI tools. The retailer said AI is helping workers complete tasks more efficiently rather than replacing them.Sharing how Walmart employees are using AI to solve business problems, executives revealed a tool developed by a freight transport manager that helps truck drivers find the best loads near the end of their workweek.According to Walmart Chief Talent Officer Lo Stomski, the tool "reduces empty miles.It saves the company money, and it helps keep Walmart's commitment to getting drivers home."As per the FT report, Walmart is also using AI to help design products, improve inventory management and support self-checkout systems. Daniel Danker, Walmart's executive vice-president for AI acceleration, product and design, said AI could help the company better predict customer demand."What I hope for, the kind of north star that I imagine, is if there's a sudden heatwave, all of a sudden all the items you would need in a heatwave are right there, available for delivery in 30 minutes or less," Danker said as quoted in the report.
Article Summary: Executives said technology will play a bigger role in the future of work, but employees will remain at the center of the business. Walmart recently announced that any US employee can now get certified in the use of OpenAI tools. The retailer said AI is helping workers complete tasks more efficiently rather than replacing them.
